Pan, Aizhao
Hu, Mingyou
Zhang, Guanjun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely used in many fields due to their excellent optoelectronic properties. However, the impact of precursor on the structure, properties, and formation mechanism of CQDs under gas-liquid discharge process is poorly understood, which invites more detailed studies toward structure design and fluorescence regulation. In this study, the CQDs of blue fluorescence are successfully synthesized using only low-cost organic solvent such as ethanol, 1-butanol, and 1-hexanol as the precursors by gas-liquid discharges within 5 min. Entering argon gas into the liquid reactant greatly lowers the difficulty of discharges in the liquids and improves the discharge stability, and thereby produce a more uniform particle of the synthesized CQDs. The average size of CQDs synthesized by alcoholic organics is 2–3 nm, and the surfaces of the products are functionalized by various oxygen-containing groups. The CQDs present bright blue fluorescence with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and exhibit an excitation-dependent property. In situ observations of optical emission spectrometry (OES) during discharges indicate that the precursors are break down into free radicals such as C2, CH and H, and then radicals recombine and grow continuously into CQDs. During the formation of CQDs, molecular fluorophores are produced concomitantly. In addition, changing the functional groups of the precursors significantly influences the fluorescence properties of the synthesized CQDs. For CQDs prepared from solvents containing heterogeneous elements such as nitrogen and sulfur show green fluorescence properties, i.e., fluorescence red-shift. This work provides a pathway for fabricating diversified CQDs by modulating the structure and functional groups of the selected precursors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of experimental setup for synthesizing CQDs by gas-liquid discharges.

A typical evolution of gas-liquid discharge in 1-butanol. The starting point of the time (t = 0 μs) is consistent with the trigger signal by the oscilloscope. The discharge modes experience (b) ignition, (c) mitigation, (d) re-ignition and (e–f) extinction. Note that the demonstrated images are captured when the interelectrode gap is set to 4 mm, while the actual interelectrode gap for the synthesis is 0.5 mm. Larger gap distance allows the discharge channel to have enough time to propagate and hence enables us to observe the developing process clearly.
Typical waveforms of applied voltage, discharge voltage and discharge current.

(a) Image of the as-synthesized CQDs-4 powders. (b) TEM image of CQDs-4. (c) HR-TEM image of CQDs-4, and the inset in (c) is the lattice spacing. (d) Particle size distribution of CQDs-4. (e) Images of the as-synthesized CQDs-4 solution under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (f) UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of CQDs-4.
HR-TEM images of (a) SEP-1, (b) SEP-2, and (c) SEP-3. (d) UV–vis absorption of separated species. PL emission spectra of (e) SEP-1, (f) SEP-2, and (g) SEP-3.
(a) The detected radicals by OES spectrum in 1-butanol. (b) Schematic illustration interpreting the formation mechanism of CQDs-4 via gas-liquid discharges.

TEM images of (a) CQDs-2 and (b) CQDs-6. HR-TEM images of (c) CQDs-2 and (d) CQDs-6. Inserted images are the corresponding crystal lattice. (e) Fluorescence photographs of the CQDs solution under daylight and UV light (365 nm). (f) PL intensity of CQDs-2, CQDs-4 and CQDs-6 with an excitation wavelength at 365 nm. Fluorescence decay curves of the (g) CQDs-2, (h) CQDs-4, and (i) CQDs-6.
Raman spectra of (a) CQDs-2, (b) CQDs-4, and (c) CQDs-6. (d) XRD pattern and (e) FTIR spectra of CQDs-2, CQDs-4, and CQDs-6. XPS survey spectra of (f) CQDs-2, (g) CQDs-4, and (h) CQDs-6.

TEM images of (a) CQDs-COOH, (b) CQDs-NH2, and (c) CQDs-SH, respectively. Inserted images are the corresponding schematic diagram of rational structure. HR-TEM images of (d) CQDs-COOH, (e) CQDs-NH2, and (f) CQDs-SH, respectively. Inserted images are the highlighted lattice. (g) FTIR spectra of CQDs-OH, CQDs-COOH, CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-SH. (h) Normalized PL spectra of CQDs-OH, CQDs-COOH, CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-SH.
Jablonski scheme energy diagram of as-synthesized CQDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely used in many fields due to their excellent optoelectronic properties. However, the impact of precursor on the structure, properties, and formation mechanism of CQDs under gas-liquid discharge process is poorly understood, which invites more detailed studies toward structure design and fluorescence regulation. In this study, the CQDs of blue fluorescence are successfully synthesized using only low-cost organic solvent such as ethanol, 1-butanol, and 1-hexanol as the precursors by gas-liquid discharges within 5 min. Entering argon gas into the liquid reactant greatly lowers the difficulty of discharges in the liquids and improves the discharge stability, and thereby produce a more uniform particle of the synthesized CQDs. The average size of CQDs synthesized by alcoholic organics is 2–3 nm, and the surfaces of the products are functionalized by various oxygen-containing groups. The CQDs present bright blue fluorescence with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and exhibit an excitation-dependent property. In situ observations of optical emission spectrometry (OES) during discharges indicate that the precursors are break down into free radicals such as C2, CH and H, and then radicals recombine and grow continuously into CQDs. During the formation of CQDs, molecular fluorophores are produced concomitantly. In addition, changing the functional groups of the precursors significantly influences the fluorescence properties of the synthesized CQDs. For CQDs prepared from solvents containing heterogeneous elements such as nitrogen and sulfur show green fluorescence properties, i.e., fluorescence red-shift. This work provides a pathway for fabricating diversified CQDs by modulating the structure and functional groups of the selected precursors.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are advanced zero-dimensional (0D) fluorescent nanomaterials, typically spherical carbon particles (graphite or amorphous fragments) with a particle size of <10 nm [1–6]. CQDs mainly consist of carbon nuclei containing sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and an abundance of surface functional groups or polymer chains, whose elemental compositions are determined by the chemical composition of the precursors [1,4]. In addition to the similar photoluminescence (PL) [6,7] and small size properties of traditional quantum dots, CQDs also have the unique advantages of excellent hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, low toxicity and high electrical conductivity, exhibiting great application potentials in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting [8–10], bio-imaging [11–13], sensing [14,15], catalysis [16], supercapacitors [17], etc.

Since the discovery of fluorescent nanocarbons by Xu et al. [18], this field has become a research hotspot. In recent years, CQDs have been an important subclass of fluorescent nanocarbons, and many studies on the synthesis, characterization, photoluminescence (PL) mechanism and property modifications of CQDs have been reported [19–21]. Generally, a major problem that CQDs faced is that their quantum yield is relatively low (less than 10%) [22]. In addition, the PL of CQDs obtained in most of the literatures is found to be excitation-dependent [23]. Still, excitation-independent CQDs are reportedly synthesized [24,25], but some evidences indicate that it is the organic fluorophore side-products formed in the synthesis that provide most of the fluorescence emission [26,27]. As so far, the PL mechanisms of CQDs have been not clearly interpreted, and multiple theories such as conjugated π-domains of carbon core states, surface states, and molecular states are still insufficient to understand the PL origin of CQDs [26–28]. These issues significantly restricted the development and applications of CQDs.

The methods for preparing CQDs can commonly be divided into two main categories, i.e., top-down and bottom-up methods [1–3,29]. The top-down methods involve the decomposition of large blocks of carbon materials (e.g., nanodiamond, graphite, carbon nanotubes, carbon soot, coal) into nanoscale particles by physical or chemical methods, such as arc discharge [30], laser ablation [31], electrochemical etching [32]. While the bottom-up methods involve the synthesis and carbonization of small molecules or polymer precursors under relatively simple and mild conditions, e.g., by heat treatment [8,11,12], ultrasound [33], microwave [34,35] or high-voltage discharge [36,37]. Clearly, bottom-up routes take the advantages of simple synthesis conditions, low price and easy availability of precursors, and the synthesized CQDs with an abundance of doping sites and functional groups. Currently, therefore, the bottom-up methods have been widely employed in synthesizing CQDs, especially using hydrothermal methods.

In the synthesis of CQDs, shortening the reaction time and reducing the cost of raw materials are the goals that researchers have been striving to achieve. The high-voltage discharge method has been gradually gaining attentions due to the high efficiency and rapidity [38,39]. By this method, plasma is generated by applying high voltage to a gas or liquid media, and a variety of high-energy reactive particles, shock waves and ultraviolet light are generated by the discharge process. These effects strongly cause the precursors within the medias to be decomposed into various hydrocarbon radicals which are then polymerized to form carbon nanomaterials [38]. Currently, researchers are mostly using liquid-phase discharges to synthesize carbon nanomaterials [36,37,40–42]. However, a main difficulty is that liquid-phase discharges are generally of a great stochastic nature and directly ionizing liquid molecules required much higher voltage than that in gas, which significantly influences the quality of synthesized nanomaterials.

Subsequently, a mixed method so-called gas-liquid discharge for the production of CQDs was proposed [43–45]. Chen et al. prepared a mixture of lonsdaleite nano-diamonds, amorphous carbon nano-spheres, and a graphitic carbon network using gas bubble discharges in liquid ethanol [44]. Shin et al. employed 1-hexanol as a precursor and synthesized carbon nanomaterials by solution plasma process with Ar/O2 gas bubbles, and investigated the effect of gas atmosphere and flow rates on the prepared products [45]. These studies show that the introduction of gas to the liquid-phase discharges can lower the threshold voltage of discharge and improve the discharge stability. However, most of the products prepared in the current studies are mixtures; none of purified products has been observed yet. In addition, the precursors are crucial to the target products, but few attentions have been paid to unveil the correlations of precursor selection with the produced nanomaterials. These significantly restrict the large-scale applications of gas-liquid discharge method.

In this work, we synthesize the CQDs using gas-liquid discharges merely with one kind of chain organic compounds as the precursor, including ethanol, 1-butanol, 1-hexanol, n-butylamine, n-butyric acid or n-butyl mercaptan, separately. We aim to investigate the possibilities of gas-liquid discharges in the field of preparing CQDs from simple raw materials and to analyze the modulating effect of the precursors on the synthesized CQDs. Specifically, this paper is organized as follows. The experimental setup, preparation method, purification and characterization of CQDs are introduced in Section 2. In Section 3, the discharge process and formation mechanisms of CQDs by gas-liquid discharge method in 1-butanol are elucidated, and the morphology, structure and fluorescence properties of the products are analyzed. In addition, alcohol organic solvents with different carbon chain lengths (ethanol and 1-hexanol) are also converted into high value-added CQDs using this strategy. Further, changing the precursor functional groups is used to modulate the fluorescence properties of the CQDs. Conclusions are drawn in Section 4.

Ethanol (purity 99.8%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 1-butanol (purity 99.8%) was produced by Meryer Reagent Co., Ltd. 1-hexanol (purity 98%) was purchased from Energy Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. N-butyric acid (purity 99%) and n-butylamine (purity 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. N-butyl mercaptan (purity 97%) was purchased from Rhawn Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethyl acetate (EA) and chromatographic column silica powder (300–400 mesh) were manufactured by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs via gas-liquid discharges
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CQDs by gas-liquid discharge method is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The main part of the discharge reactor is a cylindrical quartz chamber in which the reacted solution is contained. The gas-liquid discharges in organic solvent were driven by a repetitive nanosecond pulse power supply, of which the standard parameters were set to 4.4 kV, 75 A and 2 kHz for the discharge voltage amplitude, current amplitude, and frequency, respectively. The voltage and current signals were individually measured by a voltage probe (Northstar PVM-4) with a ratio of 1000:1 and a current monitor (Pearson 6585), and were recorded by an oscilloscope (Tektronix TDS2000C). A pair of hollow stainless-steel tubes not only served as the electrodes (with the outer diameter of 2 mm, inner diameter of 1 mm and interelectrode gap of 0.5 mm), but also the gas paths. Ar was introduced into the reaction system through the electrodes at a flow rate of 200 mL/min to form successive gas-liquid interfaces where the intensive discharges and thereby synthesis occurred.

Typically, the organic solvents of different carbon chain lengths and functional group species were selected as the precursors to prepare CQDs, and the time span of discharge treatment was set to 5-min. Six precursors were selected as ethanol, 1-butanol, 1-hexanol, n-butylamine, n-butyric acid and n-butyl mercaptan, and hereby the products can be denoted as CQDs-2, CQDs-4 (also called CQDs-OH), CQDs-6, CQDs-NH2, CQDs-COOH and CQDs-SH, respectively.

The crude reaction solution was directly transferred to centrifuge tubes and centrifuged at 12 000 rpm to remove the precipitate. The supernatant was collected and concentrated to 2–3 mL by rotary evaporation, and was then purified by column chromatography. The column for chromatographic separation was of 200 mm in length and 50 mL in volume. 30 mL of silica gel stationary phase materials with a particle size of 37–48 μm and an average pore size of 15 nm were loaded into the column. The separation process was carried out using EA as eluent. The purified CQDs in EA solution were used for further analysis. The CQDs powders were obtained by the rotary evaporation to remove solvents and then dried in a vacuum drying oven.

TEM and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) data of the synthesized CQDs were acquired on a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired to determine the functional groups by a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, US). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of all samples were recorded on a D8-Advance spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu Ka radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) in the range from 10° to 60°. The Raman spectra were obtained on a Renishaw inVia Qontor Confocal Raman Spectroscopy System with a laser at an excitation wavelength of 785 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were measured on an EscaLab Xi + spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, US) to analyze the elemental composition and chemical state of the samples. UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a Lambda 950 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, US). The PL emission spectra, decay lifetime and absolute quantum yield of CQDs were measured by an FLS 980 series steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The optical emission spectrometry (OES) was recorded by an optical spectrometer (Andor SR-303i-A) in the wavelength of 200–850 nm, to analyze the type of active particles generated during discharge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs by gas-liquid discharges using 1-butanol as the precursor
ce:section.ce:para: Repeatedly pulsed discharges in 1-butanol solution not only generate high-energy electrons and radicals under high electric field, but also may bring some physicochemical processes like ultraviolet irradiation photolysis, ion bombardment [38]. These advantageous effects can be significantly enhanced when the argon flow is introduced into the discharge region, because the intense reactions commonly occur at the gas-liquid interfaces. A typical set of shadow images of a gas-liquid discharge in 1-butanol is shown in Fig. 2 , in which a developing process in a discharge event can be clearly observed. The corresponding voltage-current waveform was recorded as presented in Fig. 3 . Note that a high-speed camera (Phantom V2012) equipped with a macro lens (Nikon 105 mm f/2.8G) was employed for the fast imaging of the discharge at a frame rate of 400 000.

The electric field between the interelectrode gap sharply increases during the rise edge of the pulsed voltage. When the electric field exceeds the threshold, the discharge is ignited and bright illuminance is observed filling the entire bubble (Fig. 2b). The discharge channel elongates, and connects the anode and cathode, causing the breakdown at t = ∼2.6 μs and an instantaneous voltage drop, while the current increasing abruptly to ∼95A. The energy stored in the external circuit is transformed to ionize the gas and liquid molecules, and abundant radicals are created in the gas-liquid interfaces to improve the synthesis. Afterwards, the discharge modes experience mitigation (Fig. 2c), re-ignition (Fig. 2d) and extinction (Fig. 2e–f). It is worth noting that when the voltage recovers to ∼2 kV, the discharge channel is re-ignited as plenty of high-energy electrons and active particles remain in the bubble after the breakdown.

The synthesized CQDs-4 using 1-butanol as a precursor via gas-liquid discharges and its characterization are shown in Fig. 4 . The brown powders are obtained without any agglomeration by purification and drying (Fig. 4a). TEM image reveals a uniform distribution of CQDs (Fig. 4b), and the HR-TEM image (Fig. 4c) further demonstrates the synthesized CQDs well-structured and monodispersed spherical shape with an average size of ca. 2.2 nm (Fig. 4d). Besides, the interlayer lattice spacing of CQDs-4 is measured with 0.21 nm (Fig. 4c), corresponding to the (1 0 0) crystal plane of the graphene [46,47].

CQDs-4 powders can be readily re-dissolve in the EA solution (Fig. 4e), presenting pale yellow color under visible light, and bright blue luminescence under UV lamp (365 nm). The absolute quantum yield of CQDs-4 is 5.75% at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Fig. 4f plots the UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of the CQDs-4. The UV–vis absorption spectrum presents a prominent characteristic absorption peak at ∼253 nm and a weaker shoulder peak at ∼325 nm, which are attributed to the π−π* electronic transition of the aromatic CC in the sp2 core and n−π* electronic transitions of surface functional groups (CO), respectively [48]. Besides, the PL emission spectra change with the excitation wavelengths, implying a pronounced excitation-dependent property of the obtained CQDs [49]. As the excitation wavelength increases from 280 nm to 460 nm, the PL emission peaks gradually shift from 365 to 530 nm (red-shift). While, the relative PL intensities increase at the excitation wavelength from 280 nm to 360 nm, and after that, the PL intensities decrease; the optimal excitation wavelength is 360 nm. This excitation dependence of PL is closely related to the carbon core and surface states of CQDs. The mechanism of carbon core states suggests that the carbon core in CQDs contains many polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) structures embedded in the sp3 hybridized matrix. The excitation dependence of the fluorescence arises from the fact that individual components of the PAH have different optimal excitation and emission [26]. The mechanism of surface states, on the other hand, consider surface defects resulting from hybridization of the carbon backbone and chemical groups (including some potential fluorophores) as the main fluorescence emission centers of CQDs. Different degrees of passivation of the surface states of CQDs affect the excitation-dependent properties of PL [50].

All species obtained after chromatographic separation of CQDs-4 are also further characterized to reveal their chemical structures and properties. Fig. S1 shows the photograph of the chromatographic separation process over time. Within 15 min, the brown solution appears distinct stratification in the column. Fig. S2 shows the separated species with retention times of 20, 25, 30, 40, and 50 min, respectively. All the separated species with retention times of 20 min and 50 min are colorless with no fluorescence emission in the excitation wavelength range of 280–460 nm, indicating that there are no fractions of CQDs-4 in these two samples. Therefore, we perform the characterizations only on separated species with retention times of 25, 30, and 40 min. The separated species with retention times of 25, 30, and 40 min are denoted as SEP-1, SEP-2, and SEP-3, respectively. The characterizations of SEP-1, SEP-2, and SEP-3 are shown in Fig. 5 . None of crystalline regions of SEP-1 from HR-TEM image are detected, while SEP-2 and SEP-3 present crystalline regions with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 5a–c). The UV–vis absorptions of SEP-1, SEP-2, and SEP-3 are similar, except for the enhanced absorption peak at ∼265 nm in SEP-1 (Fig. 5d). The PL spectra of SEP-1 show excitation independence (Fig. 5e), whereas the PL spectra of SEP-2 and SEP-3 exhibit excitation dependence (Fig. 5f–h). In addition, the absolute quantum yields of SEP-1, CQDs-SEP-2 and SEP-3 are 7.30%, 3.11%, and 2.59%, respectively, at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Evidences reported in other literatures have confirmed the existence of molecular fluorophores (e.g., polymers, soft gels) in CQDs with such excitation-independent PL properties [51,52]. Therefore, it can be speculated that SEP-1 is one or more molecular fluorophores, while it is still difficult to accurately reveal the chemical structure of SEP-1. Moreover, SEP-2 and SEP-3 with excitation-dependent PL properties are nanodots that have been carbonized (i.e., CQDs) according to Refs. [53,54].

The OES diagnostic method is utilized to elucidate the formation mechanism of CQDs via gas-liquid discharges. By OES, the degree of precursor dissociation can be detected and thereby the presence of active ingredients such as free radicals during the discharges can be confirmed. The peaks of CH (431.34 nm, A2Δ→X2П), C2 swan band (432–630 nm, d3Пg→a3Пu), Hα (656.30 nm, 3p→2s) and Ar Ⅰ (700–850 nm, 4p→4s) are clearly observed (Fig. 6 a) [55–57]. However, the groups of CH2, CH3, CxHy and other intermediate groups are not detected by OES, possibly due to the short lifetime and instability of these groups. Therefore, it can be inferred that the organic compounds would dissociate into radicals such as C2, CH and H through electron collisions in the discharges under the high electron density and temperature.

We propose a three-step formation mechanism of the CQDs by gas-liquid discharges, as shown in Fig. 6b. (i) Formation of free radicals from dissociation of 1-butanol molecules by electron collisions during discharges; (ii) re-combination of the free radicals; and (iii) formation and growth of CQDs. The introduction of the argon gas between the high-voltage electrodes allows the majority of the discharges to occur inside the bubble and on the interface of gas-liquid, and consequently the discharges become stable and the electrode ablation is significantly reduced [44,45]. The highly mobile electrons continuously collide with the organic molecules in the discharge region, resulting in the dissociation of the molecules into various highly reactive species such as C2, CH and H. These small molecules recombine to form aromatic hydrocarbon species with a ring structure; the combination finnaly form graphitized structures with ordered crystalline layers due to the extremely high temperature in the discharge region [56,58,59]. The synthesized CQDs are distributed into the solution by the rapid movement of bubbles (i.e. agitation enhancement [60]).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of value-added CQDs from alcoholic organics with different carbon chain lengths
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, to explore the generality for synthesizing CQDs by the gas-liquid discharges method, ethanol and 1-hexanol are selected as precursors, and the corresponding products refer to CQDs-2 and CQDs-6 (see Fig. S3 in the supplementary material). The TEM and HR-TEM images of CQDs-2 and CQDs-6 are presented in Fig. 7 a–d. Both of the obtained CQDs present well-structure, mono-disperse and good crystallinity from ethanol and 1-hexanol as precursors. The average diameters of CQDs-2 and CQDs-6 are ∼2.5 nm and 2.8 nm, respectively (Fig. S4).

Optical images of the obtained CQDs-2, CQDs-4 and CQDs-6 in the EA solution present bright blue fluorescence with an excitation wavelength at 365 nm (Fig. 7e). The absolute quantum yields of CQDs-2 and CQDs-6 are 2.44%, 1.35%, respectively, at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The fluorescence emission results (Fig. 7f) present that they show the same PL emission peak (∼430 nm). Besides, CQDs-4 and CQDs-6 solutions present much higher PL intensities than that of CQDs-2. Similarly, both the CQDs-2 and CQDs-6 exhibit excitation-dependent properties (Fig. S5). It is worth pointing out that, the optimum fluorescence emission peaks of CQDs-2, CQDs-4, and CQDs-6 are centered at 350, 434 and 435 nm at excitation wavelengths of 300, 360 and 360 nm, respectively, indicating that the CQDs-2 prepared by ethanol are quite different from the products prepared by 1-butanol and 1-hexanol. However, an appropriate explanation is still lacking.

The fluorescence decay curves of the obtained CQDs are well fitted with bi-exponential decay function (I(t) = I 0+A 1exp(-t/τ1) + A 2exp(-t/τ2)) (Fig. 7g–i) [61], and the fitted and calculated parameters are shown in Table S1. All the CQDs consist of a fast decay (3.5–4 ns) and a slow component (13–13.5 ns), corresponding to the recombination from the intrinsic and defect states, respectively [62,63]. The intrinsic states origin from the π-conjugated carbon cores, while the defect states are due to the hybridization of the carbon backbone and chemical groups [64,65]. The average PL lifetime of the three CQDs are 8.78, 8.50, and 8.58 ns, respectively. The slight difference of the fluorescence lifetime possibly due to the different content of surface functional groups [8].

The Raman spectra of the CQDs-2, CQDs-4, and CQDs-6 are presented in Fig. 8 a–c. It is clear that all CQDs have distinct peaks near 1330 and 1590 cm-1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively [66]. The disordered D band and the ordered (crystalline) G band correspond to sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon, respectively [67]. Thus, their intensity ratio ID/IG represents the disorder degree of the carbon material. The I D/I G of CQDs-2, CQDs-4, and CQDs-6 are 1.33, 1.19, and 1.28, respectively, which are in accordance with the experimental results obtained by Li et al. using electrochemical method [68].

XRD patterns are used to verify the crystal structure of the resulting CQDs (Fig. 8d). All samples exhibit an identical pattern with a strong diffraction peak at 2θ = ∼21°, which responds to the graphite lattice spacing (0 0 2) [9]. According to the Bragg Equation (nλ = 2dsinθ), d 002 can be calculated as 0.42 nm, slightly larger than that of bulk graphite (0.335 nm). This can be attributed to the oxygen-containing groups in the alcohol solvents increasing the interlayer distance [16]. Meanwhile, a weak peak at 43° can be detected. The calculated lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, responding to the (1 0 0) planes [69], consistent with the HR-TEM results.

Chemical structure of the obtained CQDs is analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 8e). The FTIR spectra suggest abundant functional groups on the surfaces of the three CQDs. Specifically, the absorption band of 3500–3300 cm−1, and 3000–2800 cm−1 can be attributed to the O–H and C–H stretching vibrations, respectively. While, the vibrational fingerprint at 1720 cm−1 is accredited to the carboxylic (CO) stretching. The stretching vibration of the unsaturated CC at 1463 cm−1 indicates a sp2 hybridized honeycomb lattice [16]. The peak at 1096 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O. Therefore, the C–O, O–H, and CO bonds confirm the oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g. COOH, OH) existing on the surface of CQDs, which enhance the solubility of CQDs [33].

XPS spectra further confirm the elemental compositions of the CQDs (Fig. 8f–h). All the three CQDs exhibit typical peaks at 101.6, 153.1, 284.8, 532.1 eV, which ascribed to the binding energies of Si2p, Si2s, C1s and O1s, respectively. C and O elements are the major composition in the three CQDs. Noted, the undesired Si element can be detected, which may emanate from the silica column chromatography process [70]. STEM-EDS elemental mapping of CQDs-4 (Figs. S6 h) show that no Si element distribution is observed in the CQDs region, suggesting that Si have insignificant influence in the chemical composition, morphology, and other optical properties of the as-synthesized materials. Furthermore, all the high-resolution spectra of de-convoluted C1s (Figs. S7a–c) include two peaks at 284.8 and 288.0 eV, assigned to CC/C–C and C–O bonds, respectively; while, the high-resolution spectra of O1s (Figs. S7d–f) also exhibit two dominant peaks with C–O (532.2 eV) and CO (533.0 eV) bonds [71]. XPS results further verify the presence of multiple oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CQDs, corresponding well with the FTIR results.

All the collected results indicate that the CQDs have sp2-and sp3-carbon-domains-cores, and the surfaces of the products from alcohol can be functionalized by strong electron-donating groups (OH) and weak electron-withdrawing groups (COOH) [12,69,72]. Besides, the carbon chain lengths of the alcohol do not affect the size and crystallinity of CQDs, only different surface groups content of the CQDs.

Toward a profound understanding of the effect of different alkyl chain lengths on the synthesized CQDs, we capture the reactive compositions by the OES spectra (Fig. S8) in ethanol and 1-hexanol. Similarly, only the reactive radical such as C2, CH and H can be detected, demonstrating the same active ingredients in ethanol, 1-butanol and 1-hexanol. Besides, the density functional theory (DFT) calculations are conducted to analyze their energy levels. CQDs-2, CQDs-4, and CQDs-6 are considered to be composed of integrated ensembles of fused aromatic rings functionalized through π–π stacking (like graphite) [73]. 19 fused aromatic rings functionalized (size = 1.23 nm) with different lengths of marginal alkyl chains are used as molecular structure models of CQDs, as shown in Fig. S9. While, their lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) are calculated. It is worth noting that all the energy gaps (ΔE = E LUMO − E HOMO) of the three CQDs are 3.03 eV (HOMO: −5.15, −5.13, and −5.12 eV; LUMO: −2.12, −2.10, and −2.09 eV, corresponding to CQDs-2, CQDs-4 and CQDs-6, respectively), further indicating that the length of the alkyl chain from different alcohols do not affect their chemical structures and PL properties, consistent with the experimental results [70,74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modulating the PL properties of CQDs through the precursors' functional groups
ce:section.ce:para: The confirmed effect of alkyl chain length on CQDs prompt us to investigate how the different terminal functional groups would affect the structure and optical properties of the CQDs during the gas-liquid discharge process. While, n-butylamine, n-butyric acid and n-butyl mercaptan are used as the precursor to synthesize the CQDs, named with CQDs-NH2, CQDs-COOH, and CQDs-SH, respectively (CQDs-4 in Section 3.1, which is denoted as CQDs-OH). Well-structured and dispersed sphere-like quantum dots are clearly observed of the obtained CQDs-COOH, CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-SH under TEM (Fig. 9 a–c). The HR-TEM shows a good crystallinity with well-resolved lattice fringes (Fig. 9d–f). The crystal plane spacings are 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 0) graphite plane. The particle sizes of CQDs are similar (2–5 nm), but the CQDs-SH are more uniform (Figs. S10a–c).

The FTIR spectra of CQDs-OH, CQDs-COOH, CQDs-NH2, CQDs-SH are shown in Fig. 9g. All the samples present the same absorption peaks of O–H, C–H, CO, CC and C–O groups. Specifically, the absorption peaks of CQDs-NH2 at 3200, 2052, 1550 and 1400 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of N–H, C N, CN, and C–N bonds, respectively, further demonstrate an abundance of amino groups on the surface of CQDs-NH2. While, CQDs-SH show a unique absorption peak at 730 cm−1, which corresponding to the vibration of C–S bonds. Therefore, by changing the precursor with diversified groups, we can endow the CQDs with different surface groups, such as amino, carboxyl, sulfhydryl or hydroxyl groups [75], as the schematic images depict in Fig. 9a–c. Although the precursor does not contain oxygen elements, it is difficult to avoid the oxygen (oxidation) during the discharge process, subsequent distillation, or drying process.

Fig. 9h shows the normalized PL spectra of the four CQDs (λex = 365 nm). The PL emission peaks of CQDs-OH and CQDs-COOH are 482.8 and 485.5 nm, presenting bright blue fluorescence (Figs. S11a–b). While the counterparts of CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-SH are 517.6 and 523.7 nm, respectively, appearing green fluorescence (Figs. S11c–d). In addition, the absolute quantum yields of CQDs-COOH, CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-SH are 2.50%, 5.47%, and 0.76%, respectively, at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

As discussed above, CQDs-OH, CQDs-COOH, CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-SH have similar size distribution, therefore, it can be speculated that the different PL emission of these carbon dots cannot be attributed to the conventional quantum size effect; while, it should be attributed to the different surface functional groups. Both CQDs-COOH and CQDs-OH are enriched with hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on their surfaces, however, the different content ratios of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups make the PL emission slightly different. For CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-SH, amine and sulfhydryl are electron-giving groups, thereby, the doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms would promote non-radiative relaxation (i.e., non-radiative electron-hole recombination), resulting in a reduced band gap [76–79]. Fig. 10 shows Jablonski scheme energy diagram of as-synthesized CQDs.

The OES measurement (Figs. S12a–c) during the discharges within these solutions also demonstrate the major production of C2, CH and H. In addition, CN (388.3 nm, B2Σ→X2П) and N (746.8 nm, 34P1/2 → 34S0 3/2) are detected when n-butylamine is used as the precursor; CS (257.6 nm, A1П→X1Σ) is found in solution with n-butyl mercaptan. In this manner, it is suggested that the precursors accompanying with varying functional groups can be decomposed into highly reactive radicals with diverse (targeted) elements, which is of great importance for the heteroatoms-doped carbon nanomaterials.

In this study, a gas-liquid discharge method for selective and rapid preparation of CQDs was proposed, and the role of different precursors in regulating the product properties and the formation mechanism of products was interpreted.

Using 1-butanol as the precursor, argon gas is passed into the liquid reactant such that the discharges occur inside the bubbles. The precursor is decomposed by the gas-liquid discharges into radicals such as C2, CH and H, and then radicals recombine and continue to grow into CQDs. Further, the molecular fluorophores are produced concomitantly during the formation of CQDs. Compared with the liquid-phase discharge, the introduction of gas significantly reduces the difficulty of the reaction and improves the stability of the discharges, resulting in a more uniform particle size distribution of the synthesized CQDs.

This method is extended to be applied to commonly used organic solvents and the results show that they can all be converted into high value-added CQDs. The products obtained by alcohol-based organic solvents exhibit blue fluorescence under UV lamps at 365 nm of excitation wavelength and show excitation wavelength-dependent properties. The core of CQDs consists sp2-and sp3-carbon domains and the surfaces of the products are functionalized by hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups.

The functional groups on the surface of CQDs prepared from alcohol and carboxylic acid solvents are essentially identical, while products prepared from solvents containing other elements such as N and S cause a fluorescent red-shift due to the doping of heteroatoms. The method introduced in this work is believed to provide a pathway for fabricating diversified CQDs and regulating the fluorescence properties by modulating the structure and functional groups of the selected precursors.
